what_it_does: An acceptance test for the self-coding subsystem's extension build-gate. It constructs a temporary store and node registry, runs a suite, and asserts that the gate accepts valid React-style extension code while rejecting syntax errors, non-React imports, bare imports, dynamic imports of URLs, re-exports from unsafe modules, spaced require calls, and external fetch exfiltration. It also verifies that operator approval does not bypass the gate and that rejected extensions are never written to the live canvas extension directory.
observations: Uses a temporary FsStore and discovers nodes from the repository's nodes directory
Defines four code samples: valid, syntax-bad, import-bad, bare-bad
Calls suite._gate_extension directly for AST-based gate checks
Tests red-team bypass attempts including dynamic import(), export * from, export { ... } from, require ('fs'), and external fetch
Surfaces broken/unsafe extensions through the inbox with operator approval and checks apply_extension returns rejected=True
Verifies the live canvas extension file path is untouched after rejection
standards: The decisive property test: an operator-approved broken extension is rejected and never written live
Explicit red-team coverage for AST-level bypasses rather than regex-only filtering
conventions: Uses a global PASS counter incremented by a helper check() function
Cleans up the temporary store directory in a finally block
Prefers direct assertions with descriptive failure labels
concerns: The test reaches into private-ish methods (suite._gate_extension, suite.inbox, suite.resolve_surfaced, suite.apply_extension) which couples it to internal APIs
Global PASS counter and check() helper make the test harder to reuse outside this module
Hardcodes canvas/app/src/extensions path and relies on real canvas/app being present
notes: This file is the build-gate acceptance proof: it certifies that broken or unsafe brain-authored extensions are caught before reaching the live canvas tree, even when an operator approves them.
questions: What exact rejection reasons/errors does _gate_extension return?
Is the gate implemented purely with AST traversal or are regex pre-filters also involved?
How is the canvas/app extension directory restored or reverted in the happy-path/recovery scenario mentioned in the docstring?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to prove the governed loop catches broken extensions before they reach the live tree; the file does exactly that by testing gate acceptance/rejection and verifying no live file is written.
apparent_themes: Operator approval is not a bypass
intention_signals: The file is trying to demonstrate that the system's self-coding loop has a trustworthy mechanical gate: even if a human or model approves an extension, the code must still pass automated safety/build checks before it can be promoted to the live tree.
novelty: It treats the build-gate itself as the object of formal proof rather than the correctness of the authored code, and explicitly tests that operator approval cannot bypass safety checks.
contribution: A concrete, runnable acceptance test that documents and enforces the build-gate policy for UI extensions, including red-team cases and the invariant that rejected code never touches the live extension directory.
summary_for_embedding: An acceptance test that validates the extension build-gate accepts safe React code and rejects syntax errors, unsafe imports, dynamic URL imports, external fetches, and operator-approved unsafe extensions without writing them to the live canvas tree.
intention_for_embedding: To certify that the self-coding subsystem's governed loop prevents broken or unsafe extensions from reaching the live canvas, regardless of operator approval.

symbols.does: A test helper that asserts a labeled condition and increments a global pass counter.
symbols.description: check(label, cond) is a small assertion helper used throughout the test file. It asserts cond with a descriptive failure label, increments the module-level PASS counter on success, and prints an 'ok' line. It provides minimal structure for the acceptance checks without using a formal test framework.
